O-01.name: Supply chain concentration & disruption

supplier_recovery_runway_weeks.source: erp_supply_chain.csv — TSMC critical-path supplier (18-week lead time)
supplier_recovery_runway_weeks.description: Inventory cover weeks for the critical-path single-source supplier (TSMC, 18-week lead time). Amber threshold 8w = TSMC target_inventory_weeks from ERP. Breach threshold 4w = minimum safe operating level (~22% of TSMC lead time). Current 5.2w = amber: 2.8 weeks below target, 12.8 weeks below TSMC lead time — a TSMC disruption today results in production stop in 5.2 weeks with no substitution path.
supplier_recovery_runway_weeks.threshold_rationale: TSMC 18-week lead time benchmark: amber at target inventory (8w), breach at 22% of lead time (4w)

O-01.agent_findings:
OPERATIONAL RISK DOMAIN — CONSOLIDATED ASSESSMENT (2026-05-28)

O-01 SUPPLY CHAIN — STATUS: BREACH (2 KRIs in breach, 1 amber). ESCALATION TRIGGERED.

The supply chain domain presents its most acute risk through the convergence of three factors at Quanta Computer (ODM-Laptops, $1.6B spend, single-source). Quanta's financial health score of 61 falls below the 65-point distress threshold, its on-time delivery has degraded to 88.7% — the weakest of the three single-source suppliers — and its supplier quality rejection rate of 1.2% already breaches the O-03 threshold. Most critically, Quanta holds only 3.9 weeks of inventory cover against a 6-week production lead time. This is a simultaneous breach of both the inventory cover KRI (breach threshold: 4 weeks) and, combined with financial health below 65, triggers the single-source escalation condition. There is no buffer: a Quanta disruption today — whether from financial stress, quality hold, or geopolitical interruption — results in a laptop supply void within four weeks with no qualified alternative in place.

TSMC (CPU semiconductors, $4.2B, single-source, Taiwan) carries healthier financials (score 72) and better OTD (94.1%), but its 18-week lead time against 5.2 weeks of inventory cover means the organisation has approximately 5 weeks before a disruption becomes a production crisis. TSMC cannot be substituted — semiconductor foundry qualification is a 12+ month programme at minimum. Any geopolitical escalation affecting Taiwan (which accounts for 53.5% of geographic concentration, and 75.3% combined with China) triggers an irreversible supply chain event within the current quarter.

Foxconn (Manufacturing-Assembly, $2.1B, single-source, China) presents moderate risk: financial health 68, OTD 91.3%, 4.8 weeks inventory against 8-week lead time. This inventory position is thin but less immediately critical than Quanta.

Overall single-source concentration at 62.6% of $12.61B spend is a hard breach of both the amber (40%) and breach (50%) thresholds. The two supplier distress flags (Quanta and one other in the dataset at financial health <65) sit at amber — one deterioration away from a breach condition requiring immediate board-level response.

BCM implication: The organisation cannot sustain laptop production beyond 4 weeks if Quanta is interrupted. It cannot sustain any compute product beyond 5 weeks if TSMC is interrupted, and cannot recover either within any quarter. Geographic concentration amplifies the scenario probability — a single regional event affecting Taiwan could simultaneously disrupt both.

EBITDA implication: Emergency premium sourcing, where available, would carry significant cost premiums. Quanta's deteriorating financial health may require supplier support mechanisms (advance payments, credit support) that directly impact working capital and cost.

---

O-02 CYBER AND IT — STATUS: BREACH (3 KRIs in breach, multiple amber). ESCALATION TRIGGERED.

The cyber domain is in a materially deteriorated state across detection, response, vulnerability management, and third-party assurance simultaneously.

Mean Time to Detect (MTTD) at 11 days breaches the 10-day threshold, meaning threat actors have over 1.5 weeks of undetected dwell time in the environment. This is compounded by Mean Time to Respond (MTTR) at 18 days — incidents confirmed are taking over 2.5 weeks to close. With 4 security incidents recorded this month, new incidents are opening before prior ones are resolved. This is an accumulating exposure, not a discrete event.

Patch compliance at 71% is a breach of the 80% threshold. There are 8 critical vulnerabilities unpatched beyond 30 days (amber threshold: 5; approaching the breach threshold of 10) and 23 high-severity vulnerabilities in the same state. These open vulnerabilities are the attack surface through which the 4 MTD incidents may be occurring — the data does not confirm a causal link, but the combination is high-risk.

Privileged access has gone unreviewed for 45 days — no threshold is specified in the data provided, but unreviewed privileged access over this period, concurrent with active security incidents, represents a material lateral movement risk.

Supplier cyber resilience assessment is at 0%. No third-party supplier has been assessed for cyber resilience. This meets the escalation trigger condition: 0% assessment coverage with 4 active security incidents this month. The organisation has no visibility into the cyber posture of TSMC, Foxconn, Quanta, or any of its multi-source suppliers. A supply chain cyber attack (e.g., firmware compromise at an ODM, or a SolarWinds-type vector through a supplier) would be undetected through the third-party assurance programme because no such programme functionally exists.

OMS RTO at 6 hours is amber (amber: 4 hours, breach: 8 hours) — an OMS outage halts order processing and fulfilment. At 6 hours, this is already causing measurable revenue delay in a disruption scenario.

MFA coverage at 87% is the sole cyber control holding at an acceptable level, though 13% of accounts remain unprotected — a meaningful gap given the active incident environment.

BCM implication: The combination of 11-day MTTD, 18-day MTTR, 8 unpatched critical vulnerabilities, and 4 active incidents creates conditions where a significant ransomware or destructive malware event could materialise and go undetected for over a week. Recovery in such a scenario would be measured in days to weeks, not hours.

EBITDA implication: Incident response, emergency patching labour, potential regulatory notification (depending on incident nature and jurisdiction), and OMS downtime revenue loss are direct cost and revenue items. The patch remediation backlog alone represents a significant IT operations labour cost to clear.

---

O-03 PRODUCT QUALITY — STATUS: BREACH (1 KRI in breach), AMBER (multiple). NO SAFETY INCIDENTS — ZERO TOLERANCE NOT TRIGGERED.

No confirmed product safety incidents have occurred (safety_incidents_ytd: 0, regulatory_product_notices_ytd: 0). This is the only unambiguous positive in this domain.

However, the field failure rate at 1.9% represents a 19-fold breach of the 0.10% breach threshold. This is the most severe proportional KRI breach in the entire operational dataset this period. At 1.9% field failure, roughly 1 in 53 shipped products is failing in the field. This drives the 1,842 warranty claims recorded this month — a direct and ongoing EBITDA cost that will compound if the root cause is not addressed.

The supplier quality rejection rate at 0.68% is amber (breach at 1.0%), but Quanta's individual rejection rate of 1.2% already breaches this threshold at the supplier level. Foxconn at 0.8% is at amber-equivalent. These upstream quality failures are the most probable leading indicator of downstream field failures — incoming component quality from Quanta and Foxconn is creating product that enters the field with latent defects.

Recall readiness score at 72 is below the 80-point amber threshold and within 2 points of the 70-point breach threshold. The last recall simulation was run 8 months ago — with no recent rehearsal, the recall readiness score may itself be optimistic. If a safety incident were to emerge from current field failure trends, the organisation's ability to execute a recall is undertested and marginally compliant.

First pass yield at 97.2% and a single near-miss event YTD are constructive signals, but they do not explain or offset the field failure rate — the disconnect between 97.2% first pass yield and 1.9% field failure suggests either a systematic in-field failure mode not captured at production (e.g., wear-out, use-condition, or software/firmware failure) or a measurement gap in the QMS.

BCM implication: No immediate business continuity threat from quality alone, but if field failure rates trigger a regulatory inquiry or voluntary recall, the operational disruption and cost would be significant and the recall execution capability is borderline.

EBITDA implication: 1,842 warranty claims MTD is a direct, ongoing cost. The adequacy of warranty reserves at current field failure rates (1.9%) should be reviewed — this is not confirmed in the data provided, but is a material implication for financial reporting.

---

O-04 TALENT — STATUS: BREACH (1 KRI in breach), AMBER (multiple). ESCALATION TRIGGERED.

3 flight-risk employees are flagged in R&D — this meets the escalation trigger of 3 or more flight-risk individuals in a single critical function. R&D is directly linked to product innovation pipeline, quality engineering oversight, and competitive differentiation. Loss of these individuals cannot be offset by the current hiring apparatus: Engineering roles are taking 68 days to fill on average, 7 critical roles have been open beyond 60 days (breach threshold: 6), and the counter-offer acceptance rate in Engineering is only 42% — meaning the organisation successfully retains fewer than half of at-risk Engineering employees even when it makes a retention offer.

The structural driver is clear: an 8.2% compensation gap versus market in Engineering. At current attrition (14.2% annualised in Engineering, amber threshold 12%), this gap is producing a slow but compounding loss of technical capability. IT-Operations attrition at 11.8% is approaching the amber threshold and, as noted in interconnections, is a plausible contributor to cyber control degradation in O-02.

SVP succession plan coverage at 72% is amber (breach at 70%) — the organisation is 2 percentage points from a leadership continuity breach at the SVP level. Employee engagement at 67% and an internal promotion rate of 18% together suggest a workforce that is not advancing internally and is disengaging — a leading indicator of future attrition acceleration.

Mandatory training completion at 91% is the strongest talent metric in the dataset.

BCM implication: Loss of the 3 flagged R&D flight risks, concurrent with 7 open critical Engineering roles, would materially reduce the organisation's product development and quality engineering capacity. In the context of already elevated field failure rates (O-03), reduced R&D and engineering oversight raises the probability of quality issues going undetected or unaddressed.

EBITDA implication: Agency and contract staffing costs to cover 7 long-vacant critical roles, plus the cost of closing the 8.2% compensation gap across the Engineering population, represent a material and growing people cost line. The cost of losing and replacing a senior engineer (recruiting fees, onboarding, productivity ramp) typically exceeds annual compensation — the 14.2% attrition rate in Engineering is therefore a significant, if partially hidden, EBITDA burden.

O-02.name: Cyber attack & IT resilience

cyber_supply_response_gap_days.description: Days by which combined MTTD+MTTR cyber response time exceeds available inventory cover. Positive value = production halt guaranteed if cyber incident disrupts supplier comms. Amber: 0 (response just meets inventory); Breach: >0 (response exceeds inventory). Target: ≤ -2 days (2-day safety margin).

O-03.override_justification: Expert override applied 2026-06-07 (EM-08 panel finding). Arithmetic rating: low (l=2, i=5). Override to: high (l=3, i=5). Justification: Likelihood score of 2 is inconsistent with active breach conditions — warranty_claims_mtd at 1,842 (up from 1,612), recall_readiness at 72 (amber, 2 points from breach), recall simulation last run 8 months ago. Upstream supply quality (Quanta rejection rate 1.2%, Foxconn 0.8%) is a confirmed leading driver of downstream field defects. Per O-04 override precedent: forward-looking indicators and breach trajectory take precedence over lagging control effectiveness score. CRO sign-off required next review cycle.

OPERATIONAL RISK DOMAIN — CONSOLIDATED ASSESSMENT (2026-05-28)

O-01 SUPPLY CHAIN — STATUS: BREACH (2 KRIs in breach, 1 amber). ESCALATION TRIGGERED.

The supply chain domain presents its most acute risk through the convergence of three factors at Quanta Computer (ODM-Laptops, $1.6B spend, single-source). Quanta's financial health score of 61 falls below the 65-point distress threshold, its on-time delivery has degraded to 88.7% — the weakest of the three single-source suppliers — and its supplier quality rejection rate of 1.2% already breaches the O-03 threshold. Most critically, Quanta holds only 3.9 weeks of inventory cover against a 6-week production lead time. This is a simultaneous breach of both the inventory cover KRI (breach threshold: 4 weeks) and, combined with financial health below 65, triggers the single-source escalation condition. There is no buffer: a Quanta disruption today — whether from financial stress, quality hold, or geopolitical interruption — results in a laptop supply void within four weeks with no qualified alternative in place.

TSMC (CPU semiconductors, $4.2B, single-source, Taiwan) carries healthier financials (score 72) and better OTD (94.1%), but its 18-week lead time against 5.2 weeks of inventory cover means the organisation has approximately 5 weeks before a disruption becomes a production crisis. TSMC cannot be substituted — semiconductor foundry qualification is a 12+ month programme at minimum. Any geopolitical escalation affecting Taiwan (which accounts for 53.5% of geographic concentration, and 75.3% combined with China) triggers an irreversible supply chain event within the current quarter.

Foxconn (Manufacturing-Assembly, $2.1B, single-source, China) presents moderate risk: financial health 68, OTD 91.3%, 4.8 weeks inventory against 8-week lead time. This inventory position is thin but less immediately critical than Quanta.

Overall single-source concentration at 62.6% of $12.61B spend is a hard breach of both the amber (40%) and breach (50%) thresholds. The two supplier distress flags (Quanta and one other in the dataset at financial health <65) sit at amber — one deterioration away from a breach condition requiring immediate board-level response.

BCM implication: The organisation cannot sustain laptop production beyond 4 weeks if Quanta is interrupted. It cannot sustain any compute product beyond 5 weeks if TSMC is interrupted, and cannot recover either within any quarter. Geographic concentration amplifies the scenario probability — a single regional event affecting Taiwan could simultaneously disrupt both.

EBITDA implication: Emergency premium sourcing, where available, would carry significant cost premiums. Quanta's deteriorating financial health may require supplier support mechanisms (advance payments, credit support) that directly impact working capital and cost.

---

O-02 CYBER AND IT — STATUS: BREACH (3 KRIs in breach, multiple amber). ESCALATION TRIGGERED.

The cyber domain is in a materially deteriorated state across detection, response, vulnerability management, and third-party assurance simultaneously.

Mean Time to Detect (MTTD) at 11 days breaches the 10-day threshold, meaning threat actors have over 1.5 weeks of undetected dwell time in the environment. This is compounded by Mean Time to Respond (MTTR) at 18 days — incidents confirmed are taking over 2.5 weeks to close. With 4 security incidents recorded this month, new incidents are opening before prior ones are resolved. This is an accumulating exposure, not a discrete event.

Patch compliance at 71% is a breach of the 80% threshold. There are 8 critical vulnerabilities unpatched beyond 30 days (amber threshold: 5; approaching the breach threshold of 10) and 23 high-severity vulnerabilities in the same state. These open vulnerabilities are the attack surface through which the 4 MTD incidents may be occurring — the data does not confirm a causal link, but the combination is high-risk.

Privileged access has gone unreviewed for 45 days — no threshold is specified in the data provided, but unreviewed privileged access over this period, concurrent with active security incidents, represents a material lateral movement risk.

Supplier cyber resilience assessment is at 0%. No third-party supplier has been assessed for cyber resilience. This meets the escalation trigger condition: 0% assessment coverage with 4 active security incidents this month. The organisation has no visibility into the cyber posture of TSMC, Foxconn, Quanta, or any of its multi-source suppliers. A supply chain cyber attack (e.g., firmware compromise at an ODM, or a SolarWinds-type vector through a supplier) would be undetected through the third-party assurance programme because no such programme functionally exists.

OMS RTO at 6 hours is amber (amber: 4 hours, breach: 8 hours) — an OMS outage halts order processing and fulfilment. At 6 hours, this is already causing measurable revenue delay in a disruption scenario.

MFA coverage at 87% is the sole cyber control holding at an acceptable level, though 13% of accounts remain unprotected — a meaningful gap given the active incident environment.

BCM implication: The combination of 11-day MTTD, 18-day MTTR, 8 unpatched critical vulnerabilities, and 4 active incidents creates conditions where a significant ransomware or destructive malware event could materialise and go undetected for over a week. Recovery in such a scenario would be measured in days to weeks, not hours.

EBITDA implication: Incident response, emergency patching labour, potential regulatory notification (depending on incident nature and jurisdiction), and OMS downtime revenue loss are direct cost and revenue items. The patch remediation backlog alone represents a significant IT operations labour cost to clear.

---

O-03 PRODUCT QUALITY — STATUS: BREACH (1 KRI in breach), AMBER (multiple). NO SAFETY INCIDENTS — ZERO TOLERANCE NOT TRIGGERED.

No confirmed product safety incidents have occurred (safety_incidents_ytd: 0, regulatory_product_notices_ytd: 0). This is the only unambiguous positive in this domain.

However, the field failure rate at 1.9% represents a 19-fold breach of the 0.10% breach threshold. This is the most severe proportional KRI breach in the entire operational dataset this period. At 1.9% field failure, roughly 1 in 53 shipped products is failing in the field. This drives the 1,842 warranty claims recorded this month — a direct and ongoing EBITDA cost that will compound if the root cause is not addressed.

The supplier quality rejection rate at 0.68% is amber (breach at 1.0%), but Quanta's individual rejection rate of 1.2% already breaches this threshold at the supplier level. Foxconn at 0.8% is at amber-equivalent. These upstream quality failures are the most probable leading indicator of downstream field failures — incoming component quality from Quanta and Foxconn is creating product that enters the field with latent defects.

Recall readiness score at 72 is below the 80-point amber threshold and within 2 points of the 70-point breach threshold. The last recall simulation was run 8 months ago — with no recent rehearsal, the recall readiness score may itself be optimistic. If a safety incident were to emerge from current field failure trends, the organisation's ability to execute a recall is undertested and marginally compliant.

First pass yield at 97.2% and a single near-miss event YTD are constructive signals, but they do not explain or offset the field failure rate — the disconnect between 97.2% first pass yield and 1.9% field failure suggests either a systematic in-field failure mode not captured at production (e.g., wear-out, use-condition, or software/firmware failure) or a measurement gap in the QMS.

BCM implication: No immediate business continuity threat from quality alone, but if field failure rates trigger a regulatory inquiry or voluntary recall, the operational disruption and cost would be significant and the recall execution capability is borderline.

EBITDA implication: 1,842 warranty claims MTD is a direct, ongoing cost. The adequacy of warranty reserves at current field failure rates (1.9%) should be reviewed — this is not confirmed in the data provided, but is a material implication for financial reporting.

---

O-04 TALENT — STATUS: BREACH (1 KRI in breach), AMBER (multiple). ESCALATION TRIGGERED.

3 flight-risk employees are flagged in R&D — this meets the escalation trigger of 3 or more flight-risk individuals in a single critical function. R&D is directly linked to product innovation pipeline, quality engineering oversight, and competitive differentiation. Loss of these individuals cannot be offset by the current hiring apparatus: Engineering roles are taking 68 days to fill on average, 7 critical roles have been open beyond 60 days (breach threshold: 6), and the counter-offer acceptance rate in Engineering is only 42% — meaning the organisation successfully retains fewer than half of at-risk Engineering employees even when it makes a retention offer.

The structural driver is clear: an 8.2% compensation gap versus market in Engineering. At current attrition (14.2% annualised in Engineering, amber threshold 12%), this gap is producing a slow but compounding loss of technical capability. IT-Operations attrition at 11.8% is approaching the amber threshold and, as noted in interconnections, is a plausible contributor to cyber control degradation in O-02.

SVP succession plan coverage at 72% is amber (breach at 70%) — the organisation is 2 percentage points from a leadership continuity breach at the SVP level. Employee engagement at 67% and an internal promotion rate of 18% together suggest a workforce that is not advancing internally and is disengaging — a leading indicator of future attrition acceleration.

Mandatory training completion at 91% is the strongest talent metric in the dataset.

BCM implication: Loss of the 3 flagged R&D flight risks, concurrent with 7 open critical Engineering roles, would materially reduce the organisation's product development and quality engineering capacity. In the context of already elevated field failure rates (O-03), reduced R&D and engineering oversight raises the probability of quality issues going undetected or unaddressed.

EBITDA implication: Agency and contract staffing costs to cover 7 long-vacant critical roles, plus the cost of closing the 8.2% compensation gap across the Engineering population, represent a material and growing people cost line. The cost of losing and replacing a senior engineer (recruiting fees, onboarding, productivity ramp) typically exceeds annual compensation — the 14.2% attrition rate in Engineering is therefore a significant, if partially hidden, EBITDA burden.

appetite_thresholds.domain_statement: Low appetite for supply chain disruption and product quality failures. Zero tolerance for major safety incidents. Low tolerance for cyber incidents: MTTD must stay below 5 days; patch compliance must exceed 95%.

S-01.agent_findings:
# STRATEGIC RISK REPORT

---

## 1. DOMAIN STATUS
Geopolitical exposure is at breach level, AI competitive pressure is elevated, and M&A integration is tracking within tolerance — overall portfolio risk posture requires immediate executive attention on the geopolitical front.

---

## 2. S-01 FINDING — GEOPOLITICAL RISK | **BREACH**
Five geopolitical signals are active, of which four are high severity. The signals **"US expands Entity List by 14 technology companies,"** **"Taiwan Strait military exercises increase in frequency,"** and **"Dell launches AI-native laptop line with on-device LLM"** drive the high-severity count, alongside the EU reciprocal tariff review and India PLI expansion as contributing context. The Entity List expansion and Taiwan Strait escalation in particular indicate simultaneous supply chain and operational continuity exposure across multiple jurisdictions.

---

## 3. S-02 FINDING — AI DISRUPTION RISK | **AMBER**
Three competitive AI signals are detected. **"Dell launches AI-native laptop line with on-device LLM"** and **"HP announces partnership with Google for Gemini-embedded Chromebook Pro"** signal accelerating on-device AI integration by two major direct competitors. The third signal, **"India announces PLI scheme expansion for domestic PC manufacturing,"** introduces an additional competitive dimension in an emerging growth market.

---

## 4. S-03 FINDING — M&A INTEGRATION RISK | **OK**
Three active deals are in the pipeline: **CloudScale Systems** (Integration-Month 18, USD 1,200M), **NeuralEdge AI** (Due Diligence, USD 450M), and **DataVault Security** (Negotiation, USD 280M). Synergy delivery is tracking at **84.0%** against target. The status is within acceptable range, though CloudScale's 18-month integration tenure warrants continued monitoring as the largest value deal in the portfolio.

---

## 5. TOP ACTIONS

| Risk | Action |
|---|---|
| **S-01 Geopolitical** | Convene an emergency supply chain task force to assess Entity List exposure across all affected vendor relationships and map Taiwan Strait scenario dependencies. |
| **S-02 AI Disruption** | Accelerate product roadmap review to evaluate on-device AI capability gaps relative to Dell and HP-Google competitive moves. |
| **S-03 M&A Integration** | Conduct a focused synergy recovery review on CloudScale Systems to identify what is driving the 16-point gap to target and establish a close-out plan. |

---

## 6. ESCALATION

**YES** — S-01 Geopolitical KRI is in **BREACH** status (4 high-severity signals, threshold exceeded). Immediate escalation to executive leadership and the Board Risk Committee is required.

F-01.agent_findings:
## Financial Risk Domain Assessment — 2026-05-28

### F-01: FX & Commodity Exposure

**Aggregate Position:** Total unhedged exposure stands at USD 4,940M against an amber threshold of USD 4,000M and a breach threshold of USD 5,000M. The portfolio is USD 940M above the amber trigger and USD 60M below the breach threshold — a narrow USD 60M buffer before a breach-level event. The portfolio average hedge ratio is 46.0%, amber (amber threshold: <60%; breach threshold: <45%). Total unrealised P&L is USD -47.0M, entirely negative.

**Position-Level Analysis:**
- **FX001 USD/CNY (Revenue, USD 3,200M gross):** Hedge ratio 43.8% — below the 45% breach threshold at the individual position level. Unhedged exposure: USD 1,800M. This is the single largest unhedged position in the portfolio. Unrealised P&L: USD -8.4M. Counterparty: JPMorgan. Maturity: 6 months.
- **FX002 USD/EUR (Revenue, USD 2,800M gross):** Hedge ratio 42.9% — below the 45% breach threshold. Unhedged exposure: USD 1,600M. Unrealised P&L: USD +7.2M — the only position with positive unrealised P&L. Counterparty: Deutsche Bank. Maturity: 12 months.
- **FX003 USD/JPY (Cost, USD 980M gross):** Hedge ratio 61.2% — the only position above the amber 60% threshold. Unhedged exposure: USD 380M. Unrealised P&L: USD -15.6M — the largest single-position unrealised loss. Counterparty: HSBC. Maturity: 3 months (shortest tenor; near-term rollover risk).
- **FX004 USD/KRW (Cost, USD 620M gross):** Hedge ratio 51.6% — amber. Unhedged exposure: USD 300M. Unrealised P&L: USD -9.8M. Counterparty: Citibank. Maturity: 6 months.
- **COM001 DRAM (Commodity, USD 890M gross):** Hedge ratio 44.9% — below the 45% breach threshold. Unhedged exposure: USD 490M. Unrealised P&L: USD -12.3M. Counterparty: Goldman Sachs. Maturity: 3 months.
- **COM002 NAND (Commodity, USD 650M gross):** Hedge ratio 43.1% — below the 45% breach threshold. Unhedged exposure: USD 370M. Unrealised P&L: USD -8.1M. Counterparty: JPMorgan. Maturity: 3 months.

**Key Finding:** Four of six positions (FX001, FX002, COM001, COM002) have individual hedge ratios below the 45% breach threshold. The aggregate hedge ratio of 46.0% avoids portfolio-level breach, but the margin is narrow. USD/CNY at USD 1,800M unhedged is the dominant exposure driver. The escalation trigger for unhedged exposure exceeding USD 500M is not applicable as a single-position trigger in the provided thresholds, but the aggregate amber status and proximity to breach warrant CFO attention on hedge ratio recovery, particularly for USD/CNY, USD/EUR, DRAM, and NAND.

**Trend:** Deteriorating — hedge ratios at or below breach threshold for four positions, aggregate ratio at 46.0% (3 percentage points above breach), total unrealised P&L of USD -47.0M with no positive offset except FX002.

---

### F-02: Revenue Concentration & AR Quality

**Top Customer Concentration:** 22.1% — amber (threshold >20% amber; >25% breach). Concentration is entirely attributable to Enterprise Tier 1 customers. No change in the top customer concentration figure is evidenced between the two data rows for Enterprise Tier 1 (both showing 22.1%), indicating this is a stable but persistently amber position. The 2.9 percentage point buffer to breach threshold provides limited headroom.

**Bad Debt Provision:** 1.34% of AR — in breach of the amber threshold (0.55%) and breach threshold (0.70%). This figure is derived from the data-supplied total and represents the most severe F-02 finding. Provision is elevated across three segments:
- Enterprise Tier 2: USD 28.6M provision; overdue 90d balance USD 18M
- SMB Tier 3: USD 32.1M provision; overdue 90d balance USD 22M
- Consumer Tier 4: USD 18.4M provision; overdue 90d balance USD 12M

Government Tier 1 and Enterprise Tier 1 have comparatively contained provisions (USD 2.8M and USD 12.4M respectively) with minimal 90-day overdue balances.

**AR Aging Concern:** Total overdue 90d balance is USD 60.0M (data-supplied context figure). Overdue 60d and 90d balances are increasing in Enterprise Tier 2, SMB Tier 3, and Consumer Tier 4 segments across the two data periods, suggesting ongoing deterioration in collection cycles.

**Trend:** Deteriorating — bad debt provision is in breach and the overdue aging profile is worsening in three of five customer segments.

---

### F-03: Liquidity & Refinancing / Covenants

**COV006 — BREACH (Trade-Finance facility):** Bad debt provision covenant at 1.44% versus 0.80% threshold. Negative headroom of -0.64 percentage points. This covenant is in active breach. The breach has worsened from -0.30 (prior test, 2026-03-31) to -0.64 (current test, 2026-06-30). Next test: 2026-06-30. **Escalation triggered.**

**COV001 — Net Debt/EBITDA (RCF-USD2B, amber):** Current ratio 2.8x versus 3.0x threshold. Headroom: 0.2x. This has deteriorated from 2.6x (headroom 0.4x) at the prior test. An incremental EBITDA decline of approximately USD 0.28B (from USD 2.28B to USD 2.0B) at constant debt, or equivalent debt increase, would push this covenant into breach. Next test: 2026-06-30.

**COV002 — Interest Coverage (Term-Loan-A, ok):** Current 4.2x versus 3.5x threshold. Headroom: 0.7x. Deteriorated from 1.3x headroom to 0.7x quarter-on-quarter. Directionally declining but remains compliant.

**COV003 — Liquidity Headroom (RCF-USD2B, ok):** Current USD 1.1B versus USD 0.8B threshold. Headroom: USD 0.3B. Liquidity is above the escalation trigger of USD 0.9B (current USD 1.1B), but the trend is deteriorating (from USD 1.2B to USD 1.1B). A further USD 0.2B decline would trigger the escalation threshold.

**COV004 — Debt Maturity Runway (Bond-2027, amber):** Current runway 8 months versus 12-month threshold. Zero headroom against threshold. The 6-month escalation trigger has not been breached (8 months > 6 months), but refinancing of Bond-2027 must be initiated promptly. Next test: 2026-12-01.

**COV005 — EBITDA (All-Facilities, ok):** Current USD 2.28B versus USD 1.80B threshold. Headroom: USD 0.48B. EBITDA has declined USD 0.23B quarter-on-quarter. Compliant but on a deteriorating trend.

**Trend:** Deteriorating — COV006 in breach and deepening; COV001 headroom at 0.2x; liquidity headroom narrowing toward escalation trigger; EBITDA declining; debt maturity runway below threshold.

---

### F-04: Accounting & Reporting / Audit Findings

**Current Status:** The completed AUD003 Internal-SOX [Financial-Controls] audit shows 1 high-severity finding and 0 critical findings. Open high findings: 1. Material weaknesses: 0.

**Threshold Assessment:** Amber threshold is ≥2 high findings; breach threshold is ≥5. At 1 open high finding, F-04 is currently ok under the stated thresholds. The escalation trigger for material weakness does not apply (0 material weaknesses identified).

**Note:** The data shows two entries for AUD003 — one In-Progress (0 high, 0 critical) and one Completed (1 high, 0 critical). The completed record governs the current assessment. The nature of the 1 high finding is not specified in the provided data; the Compliance Agent should confirm whether this finding has regulatory reporting implications under SOX Section 302/404.

**Trend:** Flat — single open high finding, within ok threshold, no material weakness, no directional change evidenced from available data.

---

### Cross-Domain Summary

The dominant systemic link this period is the F-02 bad debt deterioration feeding directly into the COV006 Trade-Finance covenant breach (F-03). This is not a coincident amber condition but an active breach with confirmed quarter-on-quarter worsening. The CFO and Treasury must assess whether waiver or cure provisions are available under the Trade-Finance facility and whether the breach triggers cross-default clauses in any other facilities (data to confirm this is not available in the provided inputs — a data gap that should be resolved urgently).

Secondary concern is the convergence of EBITDA compression (COV005 declining, COV001 headroom at 0.2x) with F-01 negative unrealised P&L of USD -47.0M. If a portion of this P&L crystallises — particularly FX003 USD/JPY at USD -15.6M (3-month maturity) — EBITDA headroom could be further reduced, pushing COV001 closer to breach.

Liquidity headroom at USD 1.1B remains above the USD 0.9B escalation trigger but the trend is directionally negative and should be monitored at the next monthly treasury review.

ebitda.p_covenant_breach_with_cov006_note: MO-01 SCENARIO ADDED 2026-06-07: COV006 provision impact (USD 365M = 0.64pp × USD 57B revenue) reduces EBITDA from USD 2,280M to USD 1,915M. At Net Debt USD 6,384M, the COV001 ceiling requires EBITDA ≥ USD 2,128M (6,384/3.0). USD 1,915M is BELOW this floor → COV001 breach triggered in this scenario. P(covenant breach) inclusive of COV006 scenario requires Monte Carlo recomputation — expected to exceed current 44.2% materially. CFO/Quant Team to rerun within 5 business days.
ebitda.fx_revenue_integration_note: MO-06 ADDED 2026-06-07: Revenue FX unhedged USD 3,400M (FX001 USD/CNY 1,800M + FX002 USD/EUR 1,600M) must be integrated into EBITDA demand distribution. Under USD appreciation scenarios, this 3,400M exposure is excluded from current stress scenarios, causing systematic understatement of P(covenant breach). Add as revenue vol driver: revenue_fx_shock = fx_revenue_unhedged × currency_vol; combine with existing demand_var. P(breach) recomputation required — expected to increase from current 44.2%.

hedge.var_sign_convention_note: MO-02 CORRECTION 2026-06-07: var_95_unhedged (1303) is the gross portfolio VaR without hedge protection. var_95_hedged (1050) is the net portfolio VaR after hedging — correctly lower than unhedged. var_improvement (253) = VaR reduction achieved by the hedge programme. Prior version had labels swapped, causing VaR(hedged) > VaR(unhedged) paradox.
hedge.mo03_verification_note: MO-03 VERIFICATION 2026-06-07: Panel finding MO-03 claimed treasury_positions.csv sum = USD 10,140M vs model's USD 9,140M. Manual verification: FX001(3200)+FX002(2800)+FX003(980)+FX004(620) = 7,600M FX; +COM001(890)+COM002(650) = 1,540M commodity; total = 9,140M. Model gross_exposure_usd_m=9,140 MATCHES source data. Panel finding contained arithmetic error (stated FX sum as 8,600M vs actual 7,600M). No correction required to model or source data.
hedge.mo04_verification_note: MO-04 VERIFICATION 2026-06-07: Panel claimed FX003 JPY cost hedge should GAIN when JPY weakens. Analysis: FX003 is a BUY-JPY forward (company buys JPY to pay JPY-denominated COGS). When USD/JPY spot (151.8) > forward (149.2), JPY has weakened — buying JPY at forward is MORE EXPENSIVE than spot, so the forward is underwater → unrealised loss of -15.6M is MATHEMATICALLY CORRECT. Panel confused Revenue-hedge (sell JPY) direction with Cost-hedge (buy JPY) direction. No change to treasury_positions.csv required. Sign convention is correct.

rev_at_risk_derivation.note: MO-07 ADDED 2026-06-07: rev_at_risk=9B derived from single-source spend (7.9B) × product-level revenue multiplier (1.139x). This exceeds the portfolio-average COGS/revenue ratio (1.042x) because TSMC, Foxconn, and Quanta supply premium compute products with above-average margins. Formal derivation: COO/VP Supply Chain to confirm product-revenue attribution by SKU and validate 1.139x multiplier against product P&L data. Until confirmed, treat 9B as conservative upper bound.
